channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: The Sun may not engulf Earth after all, scientists say - Phys.org
item.description:
1. The Sun may not engulf Earth after all, scientists say  Phys.org
  2. Complex life on Earth may last 500 million years longer than expected  New Scientist
  3. The real reason Earth escapes the dying sun gravity  The News International
  4. Earth may not be swallowed up by dying sun after all, say Belgian researchers  The Brussels Times
  5. Good news—we have extra time before the Sun ends life on Earth  Ars Technica

item.title: 'No one thought it was going to be possible.' A space telescope is falling out of space. This is NASA's daring plan to save it. - Space
item.description:
1. 'No one thought it was going to be possible.' A space telescope is falling out of space. This is NASA's daring plan to save it.  Space
  2. A bold satellite rescue mission came together in record time, but will it work?  Ars Technica
  3. Swift reboost mission ready for launch  SpaceNews
  4. Rocket Attached to Aircraft for Katalyst-NASA Swift Boost  NASA Science (.gov)
  5. A space telescope is falling to Earth. NASA plans a mission to rescue it  USA Today

item.title: "Pink Planet" is surrounded by salty clouds, researchers using Webb telescope find - CBS News
item.description:
1. "Pink Planet" is surrounded by salty clouds, researchers using Webb telescope find  CBS News
  2. The Pink Planet is so weird, astronomers struggle to define it. And they've just found it's covered in salty clouds  BBC Sky at Night Magazine
  3. "Pink Planet" GJ504b Could Be First World With Clouds Made Of Salt, JWST Data Suggests  IFLScience
  4. Famous 'Pink Planet' harbors a salty surprise  Phys.org
  5. Pink Planet illustration (IMAGE)  EurekAlert!

item.title: 'A completely different story': 300 million-year-old fossils reveal the first vertebrate land dwellers weren't what we thought, researchers claim - Live Science
item.description:
1. 'A completely different story': 300 million-year-old fossils reveal the first vertebrate land dwellers weren't what we thought, researchers claim  Live Science

  3. Fossilized babies of ancient crocodile-like predators uproot understanding of how animals adapted to the land  Phys.org
  4. A textbook assumption about early land vertebrates may be wrong  Science News
  5. How New Fossil Discoveries Just Rewrote the History of Land Life  Yahoo

item.title: Deep-sea denizens go years without food with clever biological fix - Reuters
item.description:
1. Deep-sea denizens go years without food with clever biological fix  Reuters

item.description:

item.title: The American flags planted by Apollo astronauts have likely bleached pure white after 50 years of unfiltered solar radiation, leaving the most famous banners in history standing as blank cloth - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The American flags planted by Apollo astronauts have likely bleached pure white after 50 years of unfiltered solar radiation, leaving the most famous banners in history standing as blank cloth  Space Daily
  2. Apollo 11's flag was knocked flat by the lunar module's own exhaust the instant the astronauts lifted off, meaning the first banner on the moon stood upright for less than a day  Space Daily
  3. Apollo astronauts planted American flags on the Moon- 50 years of unfiltered sunlight may have bleached them into blank white cloth  The Times of India

item.title: Denisovan DNA influences the immune systems of modern Oceanians — but researchers aren't sure why - Live Science
item.description:
1. Denisovan DNA influences the immune systems of modern Oceanians — but researchers aren't sure why  Live Science
  2. People on These Pacific Islands Have the Most Ancient Genome of All Humans (and a Lot of Denisovan Genes)  ZME Science
  3. Study finds Denisovan DNA still helping people in South Pacific region fight viruses today  Yahoo
  4. Genomes from Oceania offer new clues to human evolution  YaleNews
  5. Denisovan DNA still switches genes on and off in humans  Earth.com

item.title: Moon Impact 3.5 Billion Years Ago Hints at Hidden Era of Solar System - ScienceAlert
item.description:
1. Moon Impact 3.5 Billion Years Ago Hints at Hidden Era of Solar System  ScienceAlert
  2. A lunar meteorite found in northwest Africa has been shown to record evidence of an asteroid impact on the Moon 3.5 billion years ago, matching independently dated impacts on Earth and on the asteroid 4 Vesta from the same period, at the moment life on Ea  Space Daily
  3. Giant, 3.5 billion-year-old impact turned the moon's surface molten, rare lunar meteorite reveals  Live Science
  4. A meteorite sat in an African desert for years, and scientists have only just discovered what it contained  MSN
  5. An ancient piece of the moon was found in Africa, and it may have melted part of the Moon’s surface while Earth’s earliest life was beginning to emerge  The Times of India

item.title: Some climate models suggest Venus could once have had liquid water and habitable temperatures, until a dramatic transformation hundreds of millions of years ago. One leading idea is that widespread volcanic resurfacing helped push the planet into the run - Space Daily
item.description: Some climate models suggest Venus could once have had liquid water and habitable temperatures, until a dramatic transformation hundreds of millions of years ago. One leading idea is that widespread volcanic resurfacing helped push the planet into the run  Space Daily

item.title: 344 steps stood between the James Webb Space Telescope and total failure — any one could have ended it — and the telescope that survived them all now runs on less power than a household kettle, a million miles from Earth - Space Daily

item.title: Hundreds of Mysterious Quakes Have Been Detected Deep Under Antarctica - ScienceAlert
item.description:
1. Hundreds of Mysterious Quakes Have Been Detected Deep Under Antarctica  ScienceAlert
  2. Deep learning helps discover hundreds of Antarctic earthquakes coming from an unlikely location  Phys.org
  3. Hundreds of hidden earthquakes discovered beneath Antarctica — and they're happening in a very odd location  Live Science
  4. 500 hidden earthquakes beneath Antarctica, and the strangest ones are shaking the middle of a tectonic pla  The Economic Times
  5. Forget the image of a frozen and still continent: scientists have just revealed hundreds of earthquakes beneath East Antarctica, in an area where no one expected to find this phenomenon.  CPG Click Petróleo e Gás
